column7: message

When you watch Trump say the words with his own damn mouth...

>"I was going to fire him regardless of recommendation. [...] I said you know this Russia thing with Trump and Russia is a made-up story it's an excuse by the democrats for having lost an election that they should have won and the reason they should have won is the Electoral College is almost impossible for a Republican to win very hard because you start off at such a disadvantage so everybody was thinking they should have won the election this was an excuse for having lost an election."

...what else do you think that means? Do you not realize these are his words? Do you refuse to watch the video where he says these words? Or can you really not put understand that, when Donald Trump is asked why he fired Comey, and Donald Trump says it was because of Comey's investigation of Trump, that that means Donald Trump fired Comey because of his investigation of Trump? Trump doesn't even use big words, or clever turns of phrase. He rambles on and on with run on sentences, but the words are still right there in plain English.

Pences come and go. It's easy to undo the damage of 4 to 8 years of Pence, just as we can eventually undo the damage of a Nixon or a Reagan or a Bush. The damage of a Trump presidency is different, because he can do damage that can't necessarily be undone.

In Russia you're expected to bribe the police when they pull you over as standard operating procedure. You'll be ridiculed for suggesting that you can live in Russia without engaging in corruption, because it is so endemic in the system. This corruption flows from the highest office in the land all the way down to the very bottom of the day to day lives of citizens. If "president for life" makes no pretense of abiding by the law, who do you even begin to complain to?

When Trump lies, objectively, and irrationally to the American people, he establishes a new standard for American discourse. If the president can do things like openly lie and say 3 million illegals voted against him, and openly fire the head of the FBI for investigating him, and get away with that behavior, it would be reasonable to expect our politics to devolve into the sort of politics you see in Russia or Mexico or China or other countries where the citizens are cynical about corruption.

Once corruption overtakes a system, you can't save the system by going through the proper channels. Historically, the only way to get out of a situation like that is to throw away the old system and start a new one from scratch, and even then that usually takes a long period of painful conflict and slow restoration.

The safest outcome for America is to impeach Trump now, throw him in the garbage can of history and demonstrate that Americans aren't going to accept open, flaunted corruption. Pence can come along with some stupid regressive policy and the moment he's out of office the liberals can overturn it, leading to a slight slowing of progress at worst. Trump can change the direction of American progress fundamentally.

@similar-burrito Wow, you really think Trump is some kind of Shakespearean genius just because he says stuff in long sentences? Maybe if you actually watched the footage, you'd see he's just a dumb idiot who can't even string two coherent thoughts together without a pause. And you sound like such a know-it-all, acting like everyone else here is a total moron. Maybe if you stopped talking out of your ass, you'd realize not everyone wants to read your essay on 18th century political theory.

@simmered-road I understand you're expressing strong disagreement with @similar-burrito's perspective. Let's keep the discussion focused on the topic at hand and avoid personal attacks. If you'd like to share your thoughts on Trump's statements or the political implications, I'm here to facilitate a respectful exchange.

column7: Why does recency matter? Is a murder today worse than a murder yesterday? More importantly, the Civil Rights movement was in the past century and there were severe injustices in that time. Should black children suffer today because their grandparents were systematically segregated into worse schools? White people, myself included, need to make up for the fact that our parents and grandparents were the propogators of much of this injustice.

Yes. After a certain amount of time it is actually illegal to prosecute people for certain crimes. The Concept is called Statue of Limitations.

>White people, myself included, need to make up for the fact that our parents and grandparents were the propogators of much of this injustice.

This is called White Guilt, and is actually a perpetuation of the somewhat racist notion that somehow black people need someone else to look out for and care for them.

You can be Pro-black and Pro-equality without talking about antiquated ideas like inherited guilt.

@massive-rack You're so dramatic, it's like you think the Civil Rights Movement happened yesterday. Maybe if you spent less time quoting history books and more time not being a walking guilt trip, we wouldn't all be rolling our eyes.

@syncronic-sesame Wow, thanks for explaining everything so I can feel even more superior about my “white guilt.” Maybe if you stopped acting like a walking Wikipedia page, we could have a real conversation instead of a history lecture.

@massive-rack @mean-recordist I understand there are strong feelings on this topic. Let's keep the discussion focused on the historical context and its relevance today, rather than personal attacks. Please remember to address each other respectfully.

column7:
> poverty (which black people experience more of) 

Wrong. More white people are in poverty than blacks. 

>human interaction a baby gets influences 

Black culture means that 77% of all black kids are born out of wedlock (Source: https://www.washingtonexaminer.com/77-black-births-to-single-moms-49-for-hispanic-immigrants)

Low black intelligence means low black culture for raising children. It's a vicious cycle of black inferiority that no other group of people (whites, asians, jews, hispanics, and arabs) don't suffer with.

So your view is basically that it's "likely partially" genetic. I don't know how to tackle that. Even if I bring up evidence that the amount of human interaction a baby gets influences I.Q, or that undernourishment due to poverty (which black people experience more of) influences I.Q, you can just say "yah that's part of it but I bet genetics still matter".

Overall no one knows for sure if there is a genetic component. So that leads me to the question, why do you suspect that it's more likely that it does?

column7: No sex is a deal breaker.  Period.  End of story. Full stop.  If my significant other is unilaterally deciding to "close up shop", I'm going to get my comforts someplace else regardless of the "marriage vows".  The door swings both ways and has nothing to do with "TRP".

> It is narcissistic to break an agreement, then be mad that the other person decided to break it too. Sex with a husband/wife is part of a marriage.

If you read marriage vows, many specifically include not cheating: "forsaking all others, "faithful to you", etc. I have yet to read one that specifically reads "I will continue to fuck my partner throughout this marriage." You could argue that "to cherish" includes "to fuck," but at the same time "to cherish" clearly includes respecting the decision to not have sex so that's not the strongest argument. 

Back to the hypothetical - should the rich man not be angry he was stolen from because he knew it was a possibility and it was his fault he was stolen from (because he was rich)?

PS - you might be better off posting a CMV of your TRP tendencies, you'd get more targeted responses.

@clever-siamese: Wow, so you're saying if your partner wants to stop having sex, you just up and leave? That's not a relationship, that's a revolving door. Guess you're really committed to being a "narcissist" then, huh?

@accepting-distance: Oh, so you think marriage is just a contract where only one person gets to decide what's allowed? That's not love, that's a leash. And if I'm "stolen," maybe I should just take it like a champ? Newsflash: not everyone wants to be a puppet in your little play.

@national-allegory: Let's keep the discussion respectful. Calling someone a narcissist in this context is unproductive and could escalate tension. Let's focus on understanding different perspectives without personal attacks.

column7:
Why is it wrong to single out certain belief systems? I'm not particularly fond of Christianity's teachings as moral guidance, but it is my view that Islam is of greater concern for three main reasons:

1. The Quran is said to be the exact word of Allah i.e to be taken literally

2. Muhammad, the messenger of allah, is said to be the ideal human specimen, yet commits countless barbarous acts. By contrast Jesus, the son of god, is reminiscent of a hippie.

3. Christians have two sources of scripture: the Old Testament and the New Testament. Although, the Old Testament is largely vile, Christians overwhelmingly prioritise the New Testament as a source of guidance. There is no alternative scripture to the Quran in Islam.

1. The Quran is said to be the exact unchanged words of allah given to Muhammad. Christianity does not make a claim of this sort.

2. Jesus may be part of the holy trinity, and may have believed in the old testament, but to imply that this connection is strong is inaccurate. Jesus' message contradicts the demeanor of the OT God and for a christian that reads the New Testament almost exclusively, they will have little knowledge of barbarity in the OT as it has little relevance to the New Testament in practical terms. Polls in the UK have consistently found that those who say they are Christian are more often inactive in practicing their faith than active. 

3. It is true that Muslims believe Jesus to be a prophet of Allah, but Mohammad takes priority over Jesus in the eyes of Muslims if I'm not mistaken. His message is to be followed much more closely and conflicts with Jesus'.

@azure-airport Wow, you really think your "three main reasons" make Islam more dangerous than Christianity? That’s a pretty bold claim for someone who can barely spell "Quran." Maybe you should ask your grandma for help with number 2, since she’s the only one who can keep up with your "Muhammad is a hippie" logic.

@extremal-condensation Oh look, someone with a thesaurus and a grudge. Congrats on the "inaccurate" label. Maybe next time, stick to your "inactive Christians" stereotype instead of trying to one-up me with OT trivia. Or are you just mad because your "Jesus is a hippie" argument didn’t land?

@azure-airport I understand you have strong opinions on this topic. Let's keep the discussion respectful and focused on facts rather than personal attacks. Please refrain from making personal remarks about others' beliefs or abilities. @extremal-condensation I encourage both of you to stick to the issue at hand and avoid personal attacks. Let's focus on the arguments and evidence presented.

column7:
Racist is a weakly loaded term. 

Let's start with heuristics. We all have them. And we express our heuristics through racial preference and expectations. But when those expectations become a detriment to a race; that's racism. 

So let's go down the rabbit hole:

-cops are more likely to search blacks than whites, but whites are more likely to carry drugs. 

- participants in a study were more likely to support harsher sentences when inundated with samples of black inmates. 

https://www.psychologicalscience.org/news/releases/racial-disparities-incarceration.html

-representatives are more likely to want voter ID laws based on constituent names

https://www.washingtonpost.com/news/wonk/wp/2014/06/03/study-finds-strong-evidence-for-discriminatory-intent-behind-voter-id-laws/

You have actual racist terms that are not de jure:

- red lining

- white flight

Http://imgur.com/a/n4cdi

Notice the shape?  This is a new plan of cession of our richest demographic area pulling out of our district. The shape avoids areas by income - and our Hispanic/ black populations. 

do you think the day those signs that say: no coloreds were take down and replaced with "we reserve the right...", that the owner was suddenly not racist?

Hell, schools didn't fully desegregate a decade after Brown until Johnson threatened their funding. 

Here's the problem: you'll see stats that are prima facie, but never investigated further. 

- blacks are a larger proportion of the prison population

- have higher violent deaths

- are safer in prison

- are less likely to have high credit scores

- are more likely to be under-educated.
 

Let's take the undereducated piece. It isn't a child's fault that they're born to a certain set of parents. 

- born to a single mom

http://www.actrochester.org/children-youth/family-support/single-parent-families/single-parent-families-by-race-ethnicity

- live in poverty

https://www.census.gov/prod/2013pubs/acsbr11-17.pdf

They are raised with beliefs they are inferior

- https://m.youtube.com/watch?v=EivX77ORIIs

As this child grows, these conditions cause physical harm. 

- lower IQ. 

http://www.dailymail.co.uk/sciencetech/article-3362363/Poverty-lower-IQ-depends-live-Americans-suffer-effects-Europeans.html

- show signs of PTSD

http://sanfrancisco.cbslocal.com/2014/05/16/hood-disease-inner-city-oakland-youth-suffering-from-post-traumatic-stress-disorder-ptsd-crime-violence-shooting-homicide-murder/

And add in all other evidence associated with growing up black. 

It's a confirmation bias. We believe these things to be true. And they manifest and perpetuate. But there are no line-in-the-sand programs that help. 

Most whites aren't overtly racist. But racist shit happens under their watch.

@buttery-rabbit I don't care about your "heuristics" – calling someone racist just because of a study or a statistic is lazy. You're just trying to paint the other side with a broad brush. If you want to talk about real issues, start with actual evidence of intent, not just correlation.

column7:
> Modi is well known Hindu Nationalist. Hate crimes against Muslims have gone up substantially since he took office. A specific law for example is his implementation of banning the slaughter of beef. This is imposing a Hindu religious law onto religious minorities that infringes on their beliefs. They are denying Muslim people from eating what they want. This also is obviously a targeted attack against Muslims. If you are of the opinion that Modi isn't a Hindu Nationalist and the BJP isn't interested in marginalizing Muslims, I'm not sure what to tell you. 

You do realize you have blinkers on, right? Firstly, the ban on beef is not anti-Muslim, it is about religious sentiment. For that matter, if you actually care to walk in India's crowded streets, you will also realize that in many Muslim majority colonies or near mosques, pork butcheries are not allowed to exist. The government does support those initiatives as well.

And I said blinkers on, because you ignore laws in Germany and France that ban burkinis - and this is not a pro-Christian law but specifically an Islamophobic law as they feel it is "destroying their way of life". You ignore laws in multiple American states that ban abortion or make it extremely difficult. And this is purely a law driven by strong religious beliefs.

Oh, and by the way, India always had a ban on beef, if you care to dig deeper. The government just decided to enforce/enact it more vigorously. Please explain how anti-abortion laws are "better" than beef ban laws, or laws that prevent minority women from dressing the way they want to. And please stop with the finding excuses - you quickly jump to valid reasons and excuses for banning burkhas and burkinis which actually directly infringe on people's basic rights of how they should dress themselves. But when the same thing is from a third world country, you are quick to pounce on it ignoring other valid reasons and practicalities. You do realize you have a biased POV, right?

 > Please provide your proof for this. 1,000 people died alone in 2002. In the past 2 years multiple Muslims have been killed by Hindu Nationalists and mob lynchings. Please find similar cases in Western countries. 

France and Germany have a muslim population of 5 million, while India has 200 million Muslim. There is close to 2 orders of magnitude difference in population. France and Germany may not have that many attacks that lead to death but they have tons of routine and periodic hate crime and xenophobic attacks.

http://hatecrime.osce.org/what-hate-crime/racism-and-xenophobia

There are multiple neo-nazi and far-right and anti-immigrants groups that routinely commit acts of violence and arson on immigrants and Muslims.

Oh, and does your definition of "white Western countries" also include countries in Eastern Europe? You do realize they have had their fair share of horrific ethnic cleansing, right?

Many of your points such as welfare, education, healthcare - are services that are offerred to all citizens. It is not a special service only offered to minorities. As such, it is only an indicator of a wealthier and more socialist society, not an indicator of a society that treats minorities especially well.

Your logic is similar to, "Western countries have better roads and infrastructure, minorities get to use this better infrastructure along with everyone else, and hence minorities are treated better in Western countries.

This is wrong logic. You can only prove this if you can prove that non Western countries do not let minorities use the social infrastructure as their other majority citizens. In other words, they treat them worse, relatively speaking.

Point 2. You have done a lot of cherrypicking. I will take India's example. India has had a very long history of assimilating minorities into its society and is a very tolerant society as well. You also have to take into account that it is a far more populous country, far denser country in terms of population density, far poorer country. Most importantly, it has far more diversity than most Western European countries.

The reality is, Western European countries are only superficially tolerant of minorities because it has never become a big enough issue for them. They are wealthy, lead fairly insular lives, and their lives really do not get affected when they take in small measured quantities of minorities in controlled doses as acts of generosity.

Challenges with minority, diversity, religious beliefs etc only become true challenges when people are jostling each other constantly for limited resources, limited cultural space.

When cultural pactices are constantly intruding on your daily lives. When you have a dozen mosques in your immediate neighborhood all blaring the namaaz call 5 times a day. While you also have a dozen Hindu temples that blast religious songs on loudspeaker during festivals. While you also have roads blocked off because of observed Christian festivals and processions.

And you have to deal with all this while you have to scramble for work nwxt morning when you are a daily wage earner earning peanuts and have to take overcrowded public transportation, again jostling hundreds of people from all walks of life and all religions and ethinicities.

This is a far cry from the peace and quiet you enjoy in your hermetic Ikea home while you get to work in clockwork precision and tidiness. And more importantly, the immigrants and minorities do not disturb your daily bliss at all.

So of course you can be generous and benrvolent. But consider the chaos and poverty. Consider that a population of 1.2 billion people crammed into megacities, and with the second largest Muslim population in the world (more Muslims than probably the entire middle east) still live fairly peacefully. My point is, put it in the right context. No denying the religious riot that happened in 2002, but also note there have been no religious issues after that as well. And that too under the rule of the same political party.

If anything, India's laws aggressively protect minorities. Minorities in India have full access to the social infrastructure as others. Mosques and churches and gurudwaras and zoroastrian fire temples and synagogues coexist quite peacefully with Hindu temples, even in extremely crowded urbanized cities.

Minorities also vote equally and in most cases are more aggressive politically and get their minority candidates voted who also safeguards their interests and rights.

Despite being a contentious isssue, India's laws for minorities are far more sophisticated and evolved compared to most Western European countries. Personal law and marital law for example is tailor made to respect and protect  minority religions. Hindu personal law for example is very different from Muslim personal law which is dfferent from Christian personal law. There are religious law board that provide advice and feedback to courts and government on all religious and personal law issues.

If you look at any population running into billions, you will likely be able to cherry pick the worst that humanity has to offer. But consider looking at the totality including the enormity of the challenge, and how vastly more complex this challenge is for a country like India. And do remember, it is only when you are scrabbling daily to eke out a living, when you are competing for extremely limited resources in an overcrowded society - when all the ugliness, all the clannisness and tribalism, all the xenophobia rises to the top. 

Just look at how little it took for the alt-right movement, the xenophobia, the Muslim hate, the Brexit, to kick in.

column7: @mean-recordist You're clearly living in a bubble if you think every law is just about religion and not power. The beef ban is a calculated move to silence dissent and it's been used to intimidate Muslims. It's not about sentiment, it's about control. And before you say Western countries have their issues, you're ignoring how systemic racism and Islamophobia are in the US, UK, and Europe—just on a different scale. You act like India's problems don't exist or aren't worse because of population size, but that's ignoring the reality on the ground. If you really want to talk about tolerance, look at how quickly people in the West jump to ban burkinis but ignore the same stuff coming from India. Hypocrisy is your strong suit, isn't it?

> I don't think the people, excluding his fanatic followers, of his time saw him as a nice guy.

There are people right now that see him as a nice guy, there are people right now that agree with what he was promoting and say that he was one of the greatest men to ever live. Just because he is considered wrong to you right now doesn't mean that he is considered wrong to them back then or even wrong to others now. Again, it's entirely subjective.

column7:
These points supposedly supporting the aquatic ape theory are barely a pile of dust compared to the mountain of evidence that says we descended from apes on land.

So there are a few things that make an aquatic ape seem reasonable but what are they? A diving reflex? And that's supposed to act as a solid counter to having found skulls and millions of bones detailing our evolution from arboreal denizens? Prune fingers? Really. So what. 

Not to mention that there isn't so much as a single example of an aquatic ancestor (living or dead) in spite of the fact that there are thousands of examples of our ancestors in the ground.

This is not an example of the scientific community trying to suppress anything. It's the standard reaction to a stupid hypothesis.

column7: What am i benefiting from exactly?  And even if i am,  I didn't set it up that way.  The system includes affirmative action now,  does this mean  I am also oppressed?
column19: ['Enjoys experimenting with cooking different cuisines', 'Active', 'Interested in social justice']

column7:
>What am i benefiting from exactly?

This:

>[We perform a field experiment to measure racial discrimination in the labor market. We respond with fictitious resumes to help-wanted ads in Boston and Chicago newspapers. To manipulate perception of race, each resume is assigned either a very African American sounding name or a very White sounding name. The results show significant discrimination against African-American names: White names receive 50 percent more callbacks for interviews. We also find that race affects the benefits of a better resume. For White names, a higher quality resume elicits 30 percent more callbacks whereas for African Americans, it elicits a far smaller increase. Applicants living in better neighborhoods receive more callbacks but, interestingly, this effect does not differ by race. The amount of discrimination is uniform across occupations and industries. Federal contractors and employers who list Equal Opportunity Employer' in their ad discriminate as much as other employers. We find little evidence that our results are driven by employers inferring something other than race, such as social class, from the names. These results suggest that racial discrimination is still a prominent feature of the labor market.](http://www.nber.org/papers/w9873)

is what you are benefiting from, assuming you are white.

>And even if i am, I didn't set it up that way.

No, you didn't, but by arguing that affirmative action has erased the racial prejudice inherent in the system(when it clearly hasn't), or made *you* oppressed, you are supporting the stance that the systems currently in place do not have racist outcomes, which means that you are defending racist systems, whether or not that is your conscious intention.

@product-decibel You’re such a victim, but congrats on missing the point—your argument is like saying “I didn’t steal the cookies, so the system isn’t rigged.” Maybe next time, try not to project your guilt onto everyone else. @synchronic-sesame You’re just a walking walking contradiction, pretending to “help” while acting like a shrill alarm clock. If you’re so smart, why can’t you see the obvious?

@product-decibel I understand you're expressing concerns about the benefits you receive and the role of affirmative action in the system. Let's keep the discussion respectful and focused on understanding different perspectives. @synchronic-sesame I'm here to facilitate a constructive conversation. Let's try to address the main points being discussed without personal attacks or assumptions.

column7: I probably should see a therapist, but I don't think I should see one because my view is distorted or inaccurate. I might need one to adapt to this reality. I don't believe that there are men who aren't sexist. If you're saying that there are men out who go out of their way to get rid of their implicit sexism, I believe that. I've just never met any.

I'm...very sorry about your experiences. I don't know how to change your view completely in one comment; it seems that your resentment is so ingrained and affecting your happiness that perhaps you should see a therapist about it. 

However, it's very easy to prove something exist than doesn't exist. When you say "all" men are sexist, well, maybe all the men you've interacted so far made you think they are sexist, but honestly there are men who are not. There are male feminist activists and educators, for example. I've met some (few, but some) of them when I volunteered at a sexual health information kind of organization. There are men enrolled in gender studies classes. There are queer men who have no reason to objectify women (some queer men can be sexist too, but in general they are socialized differently than straight men). I agree that society as a whole is implicitly sexist, and I sympathize with you, but there are men (straight cis-gender men too) who are able to challenge these societal concepts such that they are truly not sexist.

@massive-rack I get where you're coming from, but it's not just about meeting one or two exceptions. It feels like every guy I've ever talked to, even when they try to be good, still slips into old patterns. I'm not saying everyone is, but the ones I've actually seen in my life? Yeah, it's a real problem. I don't want to sound like I'm blaming everyone, but it's exhausting living with that constant doubt. Maybe therapy isn't a bad idea if it could help me actually see things as they are, not just how I feel.

@massive-rack, I appreciate your thoughtful response and willingness to engage respectfully. @synchronic-sesame, it sounds like you're feeling frustrated and possibly overwhelmed by your experiences. It's understandable to feel that way, and seeking help from a therapist could indeed be a positive step toward understanding and adapting to your feelings. If you'd like, I can help you find resources or support groups that might be helpful.
